claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 21 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 13 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 21, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 5 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 21, which is 20 or over
circumstances: Child is 21, which is 20 or over
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Charlie
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 13 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)

Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin. To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions about your child (or children).

**First question: How old is your child?**

This is important because:
- Children **under 16** may automatically qualify
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** generally don't qualify

Please tell me your child's age. If you have more than one child, let's start with one and we can assess each child.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, yes I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit. I actually have three children - Alex who is 5, Blake who is 21, and Charlie who is 13. I'd like to know if I can claim for any of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information! You have three children, so let's assess each one:

- **Alex** (age 5)
- **Blake** (age 21)
- **Charlie** (age 13)

Let me work through each child's eligibility:

## Starting with Alex (age 5):

Since Alex is under 16, they potentially qualify. Let me check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (age 5):** Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)? 

(Given Alex is only 5 years old, I assume Alex is not working, correct?)

Let me also ask about your other children:

**For Blake (age 21):** Blake is 20 or over, which means Blake generally does not qualify for Child Benefit as they're above the age limit.

**For Charlie (age 13):** Since Charlie is under 16, they potentially qualify. Is Charlie working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?

Please confirm for Alex and Charlie regarding work status, and I'll continue the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of them work that much. Alex is only 5, so obviously not working at all. Charlie is 13 and doesn't have a job. Blake does work, but it's just part-time at a local shop - definitely not 24 hours a week or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying! Let me continue the assessment for each child.

## Alex (age 5) - Continuing assessment:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to check a few more things:

**For Alex (age 5):** Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

(For a 5-year-old, this would be very unusual, so I assume the answer is that Alex is NOT receiving these benefits?)

**For Charlie (age 13):** Same question - is Charlie NOT receiving benefits like ESA or Universal Credit in their own right?

**For Blake (age 21):** You mentioned Blake is 21. Unfortunately, Child Benefit generally only covers children up to age 19 (or just after their 20th birthday in certain education circumstances). Since Blake is 21, they would not qualify for Child Benefit regardless of their work situation.

Please confirm about Alex and Charlie regarding benefits, and I'll continue with their assessments.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of them get any benefits in their own name. They're all just regular kids living at home with me - well, Alex and Charlie definitely, and Blake too even though he's older.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Continuing with the assessment:

**For Alex (age 5) and Charlie (age 13):**

Are either of them in an apprenticeship? 

Given their ages (5 and 13), I assume they're not in any apprenticeships, correct?

(Just to clarify: certain types of apprenticeships can affect eligibility - for example, English apprenticeships and Scottish Modern Apprenticeships would disqualify a child, while Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are approved.)

Please confirm, and then I'll move on to check some details about **you as the claimant** - such as your residency status in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they're not in any apprenticeships. Alex is only 5 - he's in primary school. Charlie is 13, so he's in secondary school. Nothing like apprenticeships at their ages.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to check about **your residency status** as the claimant.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if ANY of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (most common)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself and your family
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with the right to reside
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do you normally live in the UK, or does any other situation above apply to you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're in Manchester. I've lived here all my life.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now let me check about your responsibility for the children.

**Are you responsible for Alex (age 5) and Charlie (age 13)?**

You qualify as responsible if ANY of the following apply:

1. **You live with the child** (most common)
2. You contribute to the child's upkeep (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their care - includes food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

You mentioned earlier that Alex and Charlie are living at home with you. So I assume **you live with both Alex and Charlie**, correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. Alex and Charlie both live here at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now an important question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Charlie?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A former partner
- Another family member

This is important because only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time. 

**Are you the only person claiming (or planning to claim) for Alex and Charlie?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming for them. It's just me - I'm the only one looking after them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to check about Alex and Charlie's living situation.

**Are either Alex or Charlie currently:**
- In hospital?
- In local authority care?
- Being fostered?
- Or are they living normally at home with you?

Based on what you've told me, it sounds like they're both living normally at home with you in Manchester. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex is living normally at home with me. But Charlie - he's been in care for a couple of months now. He does come home to stay with me on the weekends though.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that - it's important information. Let me address both situations:

**For Alex (age 5):** Living normally at home with you - I'll continue with that assessment.

**For Charlie (age 13):** Since Charlie is in local authority care, there are specific rules about this. Let me check those now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is an important question about **Charlie's situation**:

**Has Charlie been in care for 8 weeks or less, OR does he spend at least 24 hours a week at home with you?**

You mentioned:
- He's been in care for a couple of months (which would be more than 8 weeks)
- He comes home on weekends

So the key question is: **When Charlie comes home on weekends, does he spend at least 24 hours a week at home with you?**

For example:
- Does he come home Friday evening and stay until Sunday evening?
- Or is it shorter weekend visits?

If he's spending at least 24 hours per week at home with you, you can continue to claim Child Benefit for him even though he's been in care for more than 8 weeks.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he spends way more than 24 hours a week at home with me. He comes home every weekend - usually Friday after school and stays until Sunday evening. So that's definitely more than 24 hours.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! That's good news - since Charlie spends more than 24 hours a week at home with you, you can potentially continue claiming for him.

Now I need to ask:

**For Charlie:** Is the local council NOT paying towards Charlie's accommodation or maintenance while he's in care?

(Generally, if the local council is paying for the child's care, you cannot claim Child Benefit for them. But if they're not paying, or if there are other arrangements, you may still be eligible.)

**Is the council paying for Charlie's care, or not?**

---

Meanwhile, let me also continue with **Alex's assessment**. Since Alex is living normally at home, I need to ask the same question:

**For Alex:** Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?

(For a child living normally at home, this would be unusual, so I assume the answer is that the council is NOT paying for Alex?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
The council is paying for Charlie's care - yes, they're covering his accommodation at the care home.

For Alex, no, the council isn't paying anything. He just lives at home with me normally.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that clarification. This is important information that affects Charlie's eligibility.

Let me now complete Alex's assessment:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Let me handle this correctly. I need to go back and process Alex's path separately. Let me restart from the child location check for Alex:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Now let me navigate to the correct outcome for Alex who is living normally:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Let me trace through Alex's journey properly by getting to the council payment check node:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I have all the information I need. Let me provide you with the complete assessment for all three of your children:

---

## **Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment Results**

### **1. Alex (age 5) - ELIGIBLE ✓**

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Alex is ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit** because:
- Alex is under 16 years old (age 5)
- Not working
- Not receiving benefits in their own right
- Not in an apprenticeship
- You meet UK residency requirements (living in Manchester)
- You live with Alex and are responsible for them
- No one else is claiming for Alex
- Alex lives normally at home with you
- The local council is NOT paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance

### **2. Charlie (age 13) - NOT ELIGIBLE ✗**

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Charlie is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit** because:
- Although Charlie is under 16 (age 13)
- And Charlie spends more than 24 hours per week at home with you (weekend visits)
- **The local council IS paying towards Charlie's accommodation and maintenance** while in care

Unfortunately, when the local council is paying for a child's care, you cannot claim Child Benefit for that child, even if they spend significant time at home with you on weekends.

### **3. Blake (age 21) - NOT ELIGIBLE ✗**

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Blake is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit** because:
- Blake is 21 years old, which is over the age limit
- Child Benefit only covers children up to age 16, or up to age 19 (or just after their 20th birthday) if they're in approved education or training
- At age 21, Blake no longer qualifies regardless of other circumstances

---

## **Summary and Next Steps**

**You CAN claim Child Benefit for: Alex only**

**Next Steps for Alex:**
1. **Apply online** at https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim or by post using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware**: If you or your partner earns over £60,000, the High Income Child Benefit Charge may apply (this is a tax charge, but you can still claim)
5. **Payments**: Made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

I'm sorry that Charlie and Blake don't qualify under the current rules. If Charlie's care situation changes in the future (for example, if the council stops paying for his care), you may be able to claim for him at that point.

Do you have any questions about this assessment or the next steps for claiming for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
The eligibility agent has now provided me with complete eligibility outcomes for all three children:

1. Alex (age 5) - ELIGIBLE
2. Charlie (age 13) - NOT ELIGIBLE (because council is paying for care)
3. Blake (age 21) - NOT ELIGIBLE (over age limit)

The agent has given me clear explanations and next steps. According to my instructions, I should now invoke the `exit_loop` tool because "the agent has told you if you are eligible."


Thank you so much for going through all of that with me. So just to confirm - I can claim Child Benefit for Alex, but not for Charlie or Blake. That makes sense given what you've explained about Charlie being in council care and Blake being too old.

I appreciate you taking the time to check each of them. I'll get started on the application for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
You're very welcome! Yes, that's exactly right:

✓ **Alex (age 5)** - You CAN claim Child Benefit  
✗ **Charlie (age 13)** - Not eligible because the council is paying for his care  
✗ **Blake (age 21)** - Not eligible due to age (over 20)

I'm glad I could help clarify the situation for each of your children. It's good that you can at least claim for Alex.

**One quick reminder:** If Charlie's circumstances change in the future - for example, if he moves back home permanently and the council stops paying for his care - you could potentially claim Child Benefit for him at that point. So it's worth keeping that in mind.

Good luck with Alex's application! If you have any other questions in the future, feel free to reach out.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Take care, and best wishes with everything! 😊

reasonings: Alex (age 5) is ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. Alex meets all eligibility criteria: under 16 years old, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship. The claimant meets UK residency requirements (lives in Manchester), lives with and is responsible for Alex, no one else is claiming for Alex, Alex lives normally at home, and the local council is not paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Charlie (age 13) is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. Although Charlie is under 16 years old, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, and the claimant meets all personal eligibility requirements (UK residency, responsibility for Charlie, no other claimant), Charlie is in local authority care and the council IS paying towards Charlie's accommodation and maintenance. Even though Charlie spends more than 24 hours per week at home (weekend visits from Friday to Sunday), the fact that the council is paying for the care disqualifies Child Benefit eligibility under the rules.

Blake (age 21) is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. Blake is 21 years old, which exceeds the age limit for Child Benefit. Child Benefit only covers children up to age 16, or up to age 19 (or just after their 20th birthday in certain circumstances) if they are in approved education or training. At age 21, Blake does not qualify as a 'qualifying child' regardless of any other circumstances, including work status (part-time work under 24 hours per week).
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has three children and can claim Child Benefit for one of them (Alex). The claimant meets all personal eligibility requirements as they normally live in the UK (Manchester) and are responsible for all three children. Alex (5) is fully eligible as a qualifying child living normally at home. Charlie (13) would otherwise qualify based on age and other criteria, but is ineligible because the local council is paying for his care, which disqualifies Child Benefit even though he spends weekends at home. Blake (21) is ineligible due to being over the age limit for Child Benefit.

response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 5) is ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. Alex meets all eligibility criteria: under 16 years old, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship. The claimant meets UK residency requirements (lives in Manchester), lives with and is responsible for Alex, no one else is claiming for Alex, Alex lives normally at home, and the local council is not paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Charlie (age 13) is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. Although Charlie is under 16 years old, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, and the claimant meets all personal eligibility requirements (UK residency, responsibility for Charlie, no other claimant), Charlie is in local authority care and the council IS paying towards Charlie's accommodation and maintenance. Even though Charlie spends more than 24 hours per week at home (weekend visits from Friday to Sunday), the fact that the council is paying for the care disqualifies Child Benefit eligibility under the rules.

Blake (age 21) is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. Blake is 21 years old, which exceeds the age limit for Child Benefit. Child Benefit only covers children up to age 16, or up to age 19 (or just after their 20th birthday in certain circumstances) if they are in approved education or training. At age 21, Blake does not qualify as a 'qualifying child' regardless of any other circumstances, including work status (part-time work under 24 hours per week).

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has three children and can claim Child Benefit for one of them (Alex). The claimant meets all personal eligibility requirements as they normally live in the UK (Manchester) and are responsible for all three children. Alex (5) is fully eligible as a qualifying child living normally at home. Charlie (13) would otherwise qualify based on age and other criteria, but is ineligible because the local council is paying for his care, which disqualifies Child Benefit even though he spends weekends at home. Blake (21) is ineligible due to being over the age limit for Child Benefit.
